name: Golan Heights
history: The Golan Heights has been inhabited since ancient times and served as a crossroads between the Levant and Mesopotamia. It was controlled by various empires including the Romans, Ottomans, and the French during the mandate period. In 1967, Israel captured the territory from Syria during the Six-Day War, and it has remained under Israeli administration since then. The region's status remains disputed under international law, though Israel formally annexed it in 1981.
culturalNotes: The Golan Heights is known for its dramatic volcanic landscape, natural springs, and outdoor recreation opportunities that attract Israeli visitors year-round. The region has a strong agricultural tradition, particularly in wine production and apple farming, with several kibbutzim and moshavim established by Israeli settlers. Its character blends frontier settlement spirit with stunning natural beauty, featuring forests, waterfalls, and hiking trails that define its identity as a recreation destination.
bestTimeToVisit: May to September for hiking and panoramic views of the surrounding valleys.
languageNotes: Hebrew is the primary language spoken throughout the Golan Heights, as it is Israeli territory. Visitors will find that most establishments use Hebrew signage and staff speak Hebrew, with English increasingly common in tourist areas.

places.name: Mount Hermon
places.description: Israel's highest peak at 2,236 meters, offering stunning panoramic views of three countries and serving as a year-round ski resort in winter. The summit is accessible by cable car and provides excellent hiking opportunities with spectacular vistas across the region.

activities.detail: Ride the rotating cable car to the summit for panoramic views across Israel, Syria, and Lebanon without the strenuous hike.
Enjoy Israel's only ski resort with multiple slopes and ski schools operating during winter months, typically December through March.
Trek the marked hiking path from the cable car station to the true peak, offering 360-degree vistas and wildflower meadows in spring.
Explore the lush stream canyon at the mountain's base with cascading waterfalls and natural pools fed by Hermon's snowmelt.
Observe thousands of migratory birds passing over Hermon in spring and autumn along major north-south flight corridors.
Visit local agricultural farms on the Golan slopes cultivating apples, cherries, and wild berries unique to the alpine microclimate.
Capture dramatic evening light over the Anti-Lebanon mountains and the Hula Valley from designated viewpoints along the ridge.

food.detail: Taste crisp apples, peaches, and cherries grown exclusively on Hermon's alpine slopes, sold fresh at local markets and farm stands.
Sample traditional Levantine mezze, kibbeh, and grilled meats prepared in family-run establishments in nearby Golan communities.
Enjoy seasonal mushroom preparations, particularly porcini and chanterelles foraged from the mountain's cool forests and served in local restaurants.
Experience boutique wineries established on the Golan Heights producing Cabernet Sauvignon and Chardonnay at high altitude.
Try fresh dairy products including labneh and goat cheese produced by Golan communities using traditional Levantine methods.

culture.detail: Bring layers as mountain weather shifts dramatically; summer peaks at 20°C while winter temperatures drop below freezing with heavy snow.
Be aware that Mount Hermon sits in a disputed territory with Syria; respect marked boundaries and stay on official trails away from military zones.
Arrive early for cable car rides and clearest views, as afternoon fog and clouds frequently obscure the summit by midday.
Most trail markers and facility information are in Hebrew; obtain maps in advance and note that some areas may require special permits or closed-toe hiking boots.

places.name: Katzrin
places.description: The largest city in Golan Heights with a modern infrastructure and welcoming atmosphere, serving as the region's main hub. Home to excellent wine tasting at local wineries, museums showcasing Golan heritage, and a charming ancient synagogue.

activities.detail: Visit one of the region's renowned wineries such as Golan Heights Winery or Yarden to taste locally produced wines and learn about Golan viticulture.
Explore the reconstructed 1st-century Byzantine synagogue that showcases the region's Jewish heritage and features original basalt architecture.
Browse artifacts and exhibits detailing the Golan Heights' rich history from prehistoric times through the Ottoman period.
Trek to a spectacular waterfall in a nearby nature reserve, featuring basalt canyons and seasonal water flows typical of the Golan landscape.
Experience winter skiing or year-round cable car rides offering panoramic views of the Golan Heights and surrounding regions.
Stroll through the local marketplace to sample fresh produce, honey, cheese, and regional handicrafts from Golan vendors.

food.detail: Locally raised lamb and beef grilled with regional herbs, often served with fresh salad and local bread at traditional restaurants.
Creamy or tangy artisanal goat cheese produced locally, often served as a meze with olive oil and fresh herbs.
Seasonal fresh apples, plums, and peaches grown at high altitude in the Golan, known for exceptional sweetness and flavor.
Vegetables cooked on heated basalt stones, a traditional Golan cooking method that imparts a distinctive smoky flavor.
Local wildflower honey from the Golan's diverse flora, often sampled at markets and used in traditional desserts.

culture.detail: The ancient synagogue and other religious sites are active places of worship; dress modestly and be quiet when visiting during prayer times.
While English is spoken at major tourist sites, learning basic Hebrew greetings shows respect and enhances interactions with local shop owners and residents.
Many local shops, restaurants, and services close or have limited hours on Friday afternoon through Saturday evening; plan activities accordingly.
Be aware that the Golan Heights' political status is sensitive; avoid discussing contentious topics and respect local perspectives on the region's history.

places.name: Banias Waterfall and Spring
places.description: A spectacular natural spring and waterfall cascading through lush green canyons with ancient archaeological ruins nestled within. The scenic hiking trail along the stream offers refreshing pools, verdant vegetation, and glimpses of Crusader castle ruins.

activities.detail: Follow the main trail along the Banias Stream through the canyon to reach the dramatic 50-meter waterfall cascading into a refreshing natural pool.
Cool off in the crystal-clear emerald pools formed by the spring water throughout the hiking route, particularly pleasant during summer months.
Visit the medieval Banias Castle ruins perched on the hillside overlooking the springs, dating back to the 12th century.
Trek along the scenic stream path surrounded by dense vegetation, wildflowers, and hanging gardens unique to this water-fed landscape.
Discover remnants of the Pan grotto sanctuary and Hellenistic temple structures nestled at the spring's source.
Capture stunning images of the waterfall, cascading streams, verdant canyon walls, and archaeological sites throughout the reserve.
Enjoy a meal on designated spots near the stream with views of the natural pools and surrounding green landscape.

food.detail: Locally caught fish from the pristine spring-fed waters of the region, often grilled or served at nearby kibbutz restaurants.
Creamy chickpea dip served warm with olive oil and fresh pita bread, a staple of Golan Heights cuisine available at nearby dining establishments.
Tangy locally-produced cheese from regional farms, often served fresh or aged and paired with local vegetables.
Eggs poached in tomato sauce with herbs grown in the fertile Golan valleys, a hearty traditional breakfast dish.
Fresh salad incorporating herbs and vegetables available at visitor areas, celebrating the local produce of the water-rich region.

culture.detail: Do not remove artifacts or climb on ancient ruins; these sites are protected cultural heritage from Hellenistic, Crusader, and other periods.
Be aware that water levels and pool temperatures vary seasonally; winter brings higher flows and colder temperatures while summer offers warmer, more accessible pools.
The area is disputed territory; visitors should be aware of the geopolitical situation and follow local guidance regarding safe areas to visit.
Pack out all trash and stay on marked trails to preserve the delicate canyon ecosystem and protect the natural springs that make this area unique.

places.name: Hula Valley Nature Reserve
places.description: One of the most important birdwatching locations in the world, hosting hundreds of thousands of migratory birds during spring and fall. The wetland reserve offers visitor centers, observation towers, and scenic trails through pristine natural habitat.

activities.detail: Climb the multiple elevated towers throughout the reserve to spot migrating warblers, herons, storks, and eagles during spring and fall migrations.
Follow marked paths through reed beds and open water areas where you can observe waterfowl and wetland ecosystems up close.

activities.text: Visit the Hula Valley Visitor Center
activities.detail: Explore interactive exhibits about the valley's ecology, restoration history, and migratory bird species with expert staff guidance.
Use dedicated photography blinds positioned along water channels to capture intimate shots of birds without disturbing them.
Experience thousands of birds returning to roost at dusk, creating spectacular aerial displays and photographic opportunities.
Rent bikes to cover more ground while moving quietly through the wetland corridors, ideal for spotting shy species.

food.text: Fresh fish from Kibbutz Ein Gev
food.detail: Sample grilled St. Peter's fish (tilapia) caught from the Sea of Galilee nearby, a regional specialty prepared simply with lemon and herbs.
Purchase fresh vegetables grown in the kibbutzim surrounding the valley, including tomatoes, cucumbers, and peppers sold at local farm stands.
Enjoy the regional kibbutz breakfast tradition with cheese, yogurt, fresh bread, salads, and eggs at guesthouses in nearby settlements.
Try traditional Galilean fish soup made with freshwater fish from local ponds, seasoned with Middle Eastern spices and served with bread.

culture.text: Visit during migration seasons for best birdwatching
culture.detail: Plan your visit between March-May (spring) or August-October (fall) when hundreds of thousands of migratory birds pass through the valley.

culture.text: Respect quiet hours and wildlife protocols
culture.detail: Keep noise minimal and stay on designated paths to avoid disturbing nesting and feeding birds, as the reserve is a protected nature sanctuary.

culture.text: Learn about the Hula reclamation story
culture.detail: Understand the valley's unique history where drained swampland (1950s-1970s) was partially reflooded in the 1990s to restore critical wetland habitat.

culture.text: Bring binoculars and a bird guidebook
culture.detail: Come prepared with quality optics and a field guide to identify species, as this is a serious birdwatching destination attracting expert ornithologists worldwide.

places.name: Gamla
places.description: An ancient Jewish city perched dramatically on a narrow ridge with sweeping views of the surrounding valleys and Sea of Galilee. The archaeological site combines fascinating First Temple-era ruins with spectacular hiking along clifftop trails and beneath soaring griffon vultures.

activities.detail: Hike the narrow, dramatic ridgeline that forms the backbone of the ancient city, offering sweeping 360-degree views of the Yarmouk River valley and Sea of Galilee below.
Wander through excavated stone foundations, cisterns, and defensive walls dating to the 8th century BCE, with interpretive markers explaining the layout of this strategic hilltop settlement.
Watch these massive raptors circle and nest on the cliff faces surrounding Gamla, particularly visible during spring and autumn migration periods.
Hike down the steep trail beneath Gamla to reach a seasonal waterfall and pools fed by springs in the Yarmouk River canyon, offering relief on hot days.
Study artifacts and pottery shards recovered from the site, along with detailed explanations of daily life during the Second Temple period and the Roman siege.
Climb to the highest vantage on the ridge for panoramic shots of the volcanic landscape, the Golan plateau, and the valley stretching toward Syria and Jordan.

food.text: Druze Pita with Za'atar and Olive Oil
food.detail: Warm flatbread from local Druze communities in nearby villages, traditionally served with fragrant za'atar herb mix and locally pressed olive oil for dipping.

food.text: Grilled Fresh Trout from Local Springs
food.detail: Rainbow trout caught from springs and streams in the Golan, grilled simply with lemon and fresh herbs, reflecting the region's abundant freshwater sources.
Thick, tangy strained yogurt served alongside crisp cucumbers, tomatoes, and wild herbs foraged from the surrounding volcanic plateau.
Young artichokes gathered from Golan farms, roasted over open fire or in local ovens with abundant garlic, olive oil, and Druze spices.

culture.text: Wear sturdy hiking boots and bring plenty of water
culture.detail: The narrow ridgeline is steep with loose volcanic rock, and there is virtually no shade; summer temperatures exceed 40°C and the nearest services are in Kibbutz Ein Zivan or Katzrin.
Stay on marked trails and do not remove artifacts or disturb excavation sites, as Gamla is an active archaeological area with ongoing research and conservation.

culture.text: Visit early morning or late afternoon
culture.detail: The exposed ridge becomes dangerously hot by midday; dawn and sunset also offer the best lighting for photography and wildlife observation, especially for vultures.

culture.text: Learn about the Roman siege history
culture.detail: Gamla fell to Roman forces under Vespasian in 67 CE after a famous siege; understanding this tragic context enriches appreciation for the ruins and the site's significance to Jewish history.

places.name: Sea of Galilee (Eastern Shore)
places.description: The freshwater lake bordering Golan Heights offers tranquil beaches, water sports, and historic fishing villages along its eastern shore. Visitors can enjoy swimming, windsurfing, fishing, or simply relaxing while taking in views of the surrounding mountains.
imageQueries: Sea of Galilee eastern shore Israel

activities.detail: A popular sandy beach with clear shallow waters, facilities, and views across to the western shore and Galilee hills.
Strong thermal winds in the afternoon make the eastern shore ideal for windsurfing, sailing, and jet skiing during peak season.

activities.text: Visit Ein Gev Kibbutz and Fish Ponds
activities.detail: Tour the historic kibbutz settlement with functioning tilapia and carp farms, and learn about communal agricultural history.
Cast for native tilapia and carp year-round, or charter a fishing boat for deeper-water angling experiences.
Access trails from the shore leading into the Golan with panoramic views of the lake, volcanic terrain, and Syria across the border.
Paddle through coves and inlets to explore hidden beaches and observe waterfowl in a quieter, slower-paced way.

food.detail: The lake's signature dish, traditionally grilled whole or filleted, often served with lemon and herbs at waterfront restaurants.
A Jewish-Eastern European preparation using fresh carp from the lake's farms, poached and served cold with horseradish.
A communal-style meal of fresh vegetables, cheeses, breads, eggs, and olives reflecting the kibbutz agricultural tradition.
Ask restaurants about the daily catch to enjoy locally-netted fish prepared simply grilled or in fish soups.
Sample wines from nearby Golan Heights vineyards at restaurants and tasting rooms, often paired with local fish dishes.

culture.detail: The eastern shore borders Syria and is a sensitive military zone; follow all local signage and avoid photographing military installations.
If visiting kibbutz grounds or eating at kibbutz restaurants, remember these are working communities—respect private areas and scheduled visiting hours.
While secular, the area has religious Jewish visitors; swimwear is standard at beaches but cover-ups are appreciated in village areas.
The region is Hebrew-speaking with some Arabic; basic Hebrew phrases are helpful, and signs may appear in both languages reflecting the diverse regional history.

places.name: Q Restaurant and Winery
places.description: A renowned winery and restaurant showcasing Golan Heights wines in a modern tasting room with panoramic vineyard views. The combination of award-winning wines, gourmet cuisine, and warm hospitality makes it a must-visit destination for wine enthusiasts.

activities.detail: Sample Q Winery's flagship Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, and white varieties in the modern tasting room while learning about Golan Heights terroir from knowledgeable sommeliers.
Stroll through the surrounding vineyard rows on the property to experience the volcanic basalt soil and panoramic views of the Golan Heights landscape that define the region's wine character.
Enjoy a multi-course dinner at Q Restaurant paired with estate wines, featuring dishes designed to complement the winery's specific vintages and production philosophy.
From Q's terrace, observe Mount Hermon on the horizon and the dramatic volcanic plateau geography that influences the cool-climate wine production in this elevated region.
Participate in guided tastings that explain the unique characteristics of high-altitude Golan Heights wines and compare local varietals with international standards.

food.detail: Locally-sourced lamb prepared with Q's proprietary red wine reduction, reflecting the region's pastoral heritage and showcase of house vintages in cuisine.

food.text: Goat Cheese and Vineyard Greens Salad
food.detail: Fresh local goat cheese paired with seasonal greens from Golan Heights farms, dressed with estate olive oil and light vinaigrette.
Seasonal roasted vegetables from regional producers, charred in a wood-fired oven and dressed with herbs that complement Q's white wine selections.
Premium beef cooked on heated volcanic basalt stones from the Golan plateau, a technique honoring the terroir while pairing with bold red wines.

culture.detail: Q observes traditional Friday evening closures and reduced Saturday hours for Shabbat; plan visits accordingly and call ahead for holiday schedules.
The Golan Heights sit at 1,000+ meters elevation with cooler evenings and intense sun exposure; bring layers and sunscreen even in summer months.
Staff may greet guests in Hebrew with warm Arabic-influenced hospitality traditions; learning basic Hebrew phrases enhances cultural respect in Israeli establishments.

places.name: Majdal Shams
places.description: A charming Druze village nestled beneath Mount Hermon known for its authentic cuisine and warm Druze hospitality. The village features traditional architecture, excellent local restaurants serving Druze specialties, and proximity to hiking trails and natural attractions.

activities.detail: Trek the slopes of Mount Hermon directly above the village, offering panoramic views of the Golan Heights and Anti-Lebanon mountains.
Stroll through the narrow stone-paved streets lined with traditional Druze architecture, local shops, and welcoming residents.
Explore the small local museum showcasing Druze traditions, crafts, and the village's history within the Golan Heights context.
Visit the surrounding apple and apricot orchards that define the local agricultural landscape, especially beautiful during spring blossoms.
Trek through the nearby Hermon Stream Nature Reserve featuring waterfalls, cool pools, and lush vegetation fed by Mount Hermon's springs.
Watch the sunset from the elevated eastern edge of Majdal Shams overlooking the Syrian plain and distant mountains.

food.detail: Freshly baked flatbread topped with za'atar (wild thyme mix), olive oil, and sesame seeds, a staple at local bakeries.
Raw minced lamb mixed with bulgur wheat and spices, served cold as a traditional Druze appetizer found in family restaurants.
Creamy chickpea hummus topped with spiced ground lamb and pine nuts, a hearty version unique to Druze preparations.
A communal spread of fresh cheeses, olives, eggs, bread, and wild herbs typical of morning meals at local guesthouses and restaurants.
Tender grape leaves rolled with rice, meat, and spices, reflecting the village's agricultural heritage and traditional cooking methods.

culture.detail: Majdal Shams is a devout Druze community; dress modestly and avoid visiting during major Druze religious holidays or Friday gatherings.
Expect warm invitations to tea and conversation; accepting such offers is appreciated and reflects respect for local traditions.
While Arabic and Hebrew are spoken, learning basic Arabic greetings like 'Marhaba' (hello) shows respect and opens doors with local residents.

places.name: Drekish Waterfall
places.description: A hidden gem featuring a picturesque waterfall in a narrow canyon with emerald pools ideal for swimming and cooling off. The moderate hiking trail through the canyon offers solitude and natural beauty with lush vegetation and dramatic cliff formations.

activities.detail: Wade into the emerald pools beneath Drekish Falls for a refreshing dip in crystal-clear water surrounded by canyon walls.
Follow the moderate marked trail through the narrow canyon, navigating between boulders and lush vegetation for about 2-3 hours round trip.
Climb over smooth stones and small rock formations along the streambed to reach deeper sections of the canyon and hidden pools.
Capture dramatic cliff formations, cascading water, and verdant canyon vegetation that create stunning natural compositions throughout the hike.
Pack a lunch and relax beside the flowing water in shaded alcoves created by overhanging vegetation and rock outcrops.
Observe native Golan Heights species including Syrian woodpeckers and eagles in the canyon's diverse ecosystem.

food.detail: Sample creamy hummus made fresh at nearby kibbutz restaurants in the Golan Heights, often served with warm pita and olive oil.
Enjoy locally-caught freshwater trout grilled simply with herbs, reflecting the region's agricultural traditions.
Order assorted small plates of baba ganoush, tabbouleh, Israeli salad, and pickled vegetables at Golan Heights restaurants.

food.text: Fresh Goat Cheese from Golan Dairies
food.detail: Try the region's signature creamy goat cheese products, often available at local markets and farm stands in the area.

culture.detail: The canyon offers no facilities or shade for long periods, so carry at least 2-3 liters per person for the moderate hike.
Flash floods can occur in narrow canyons during or after heavy winter rains, so verify conditions with local authorities beforehand.
Many local restaurants and services in the Golan observe Shabbat (Friday evening to Saturday evening), so plan dining accordingly.
Bring sturdy hiking shoes with good grip as the streambed rocks are smooth and slippery when wet.

places.name: Ramat Magshimim (Yonatan Airport)
places.description: A scenic overlook and landing point for paragliders offering breathtaking views of the Jordan Valley, Hermon, and surrounding peaks. The site combines adventure sports with stunning photography opportunities and is named after a historic Israeli rescue operation.

activities.detail: Launch from the ridge with thermals rising from the Jordan Valley below, offering flights of 1-3 hours with views stretching to the Dead Sea and Hermon peaks.
Arrive before dawn to capture the Jordan Valley awakening with golden light illuminating the Hermon snowcaps and Syrian highlands.
Scan the northern horizon with binoculars to observe Mount Hermon's three peaks and seasonal snow coverage across the Lebanese and Syrian borders.
On clear days, identify the Dead Sea's surface shimmering 1,100 meters below the Golan plateau, with Jordan's Moab mountains visible beyond.
Explore the memorial and interpretive markers honoring Operation Priha (Flower), the 1970 rescue of a downed pilot that inspired the airport's namesake.
Watch for golden eagles, griffon vultures, and steppe buzzards riding thermals in spring and autumn migration seasons.

food.detail: Thin, wood-fired flatbread coated with za'atar, olive oil, and sumac from Druze villages in the lower Golan, served warm at nearby roadside stands.
Local halloumi-style cheese grilled over open flames and drizzled with thyme honey, a staple at Golan farm restaurants and picnic areas.
Pure glacial runoff from Mount Hermon mixed with fresh mint leaves, sold at visitor centers as a refreshing highland tradition.
Sample pressing-season extra virgin oil from kibbutzim and moshavim surrounding the plateau, often paired with fresh pita.
A Druze specialty slow-cooked with mountain herbs and served at family-run restaurants in Majdal Shams, reflecting local hunting traditions.

culture.detail: The site is an active paragliding airport with military airspace restrictions; stay in designated viewing areas and do not interfere with launching or landing pilots.
The plateau sits at 1,000+ meters with consistent strong winds; bring a windproof jacket and secure any loose items, even in summer.
The airport commemorates the 1976 Entebbe rescue commander; reading about Operation Priha before visiting deepens appreciation for the site's historical significance.
Thermal conditions peak in late morning, so arrive by 6 a.m. to witness the organized launch sequence and avoid crowding afternoon visitors.

places.name: Ein Gev
places.description: A kibbutz on the Sea of Galilee's eastern shore famous for its annual music festival and fresh fish restaurants. The village combines cultural attractions with beach access, scenic lake views, and a welcoming kibbutz experience.
imageQueries: Ein Gev kibbutz Sea of Galilee

activities.detail: Waterfront dining establishment famous for St. Peter's fish (tilapia) grilled fresh from the Sea of Galilee, a signature local experience.
Public swimming area on the Sea of Galilee shore with sandy access, water sports rental, and views across to the western Galilee hills.
Annual spring festival (typically March-April) featuring classical music performances in the kibbutz amphitheater and outdoor venues.
Guided walk through the working kibbutz to learn about its history since 1937, agricultural practices, and communal lifestyle.
Fishing and sightseeing cruises departing from Ein Gev marina offering sunset views and opportunities to see biblical sites from the water.
Access point for trails along the eastern shore of the Sea of Galilee with views of the Golan Heights and surrounding landscape.

food.detail: Whole grilled tilapia caught fresh from the Sea of Galilee, typically served with lemon and olive oil at waterfront restaurants.
Traditional Galilean fish soup made with fresh catch, vegetables, and spices, served hot in local kibbutz kitchens.
Communal-style meal featuring fresh vegetables, cheeses, eggs, olives, bread, and local dairy products reflecting kibbutz agricultural heritage.
Another prized freshwater fish from the Galilee prepared simply grilled or baked with herbs and served at Ein Gev restaurants.
Vegetable salads made from kibbutz-grown tomatoes, cucumbers, peppers, and herbs, reflecting the settlement's agricultural focus.

culture.detail: Respect quiet hours in the early morning and evening, dress modestly when touring residential areas, and ask permission before photographing residents.
The Music Festival (spring) draws large crowds; book accommodations and restaurants well in advance if visiting during this period.
Ein Gev was established in a strategically vulnerable location and residents take pride in their resilience; show genuine interest when learning about kibbutz history and defense.
The kibbutz community values discussion of sustainable farming and local produce; dining at kibbutz restaurants directly supports these agricultural efforts.

places.name: Golan Wine Valley
places.description: A thriving wine region producing world-class Israeli wines with dozens of wineries open for tastings and tours. The rolling vineyards and sunny climate create ideal conditions for viticulture, and numerous tasting rooms offer wine education paired with local cuisine.

activities.detail: Visit Israel's largest winery, founded in 1983, for guided tastings of their flagship Cabernet Sauvignon and Chardonnay in a modern facility overlooking the vineyards.
Sample boutique wines at this family-owned winery known for Merlot and Syrah, with a intimate tasting room and views across the valley.
Stroll through rolling terraced vineyards during harvest season to understand the terroir and winemaking process from vine to glass.
Drive through the Golan wine region with stops at multiple tasting rooms while enjoying panoramic views of Mount Hermon and the Hula Valley.
Attend the seasonal wine festival in nearby Katzrin (typically spring and autumn) featuring local producers, food vendors, and live music.
Explore this organic winery's estate, taste natural wines made with minimal intervention, and learn about sustainable viticulture practices.

food.text: Local Goat Cheese with Wine Pairing
food.detail: Sample creamy or aged goat cheese produced from Golan dairies, paired with crisp white wines or light reds for a traditional Levantine experience.
Enjoy charcoal-grilled lamb or chicken kebab seasoned with za'atar and fresh herbs harvested from the surrounding Golan plateau.
Taste seasonal salad made with mushrooms foraged from the Golan Heights, dressed with local olive oil and paired with white wine.
Break bread with traditional Arab flatbread and strained yogurt (labneh) prepared by local Druze communities, served alongside wine tastings.

food.text: Roasted Local Fish from Lake Kinneret
food.detail: Sample fresh St. Peter's fish or carp caught from nearby Lake Kinneret, grilled simply with lemon and herbs, paired with Golan rosés.

culture.detail: Wear comfortable walking shoes, sunscreen, and a hat, as the vineyards involve outdoor exposure and the elevation can intensify UV rays.
Reserve winery tours and tastings in advance, especially during weekends and harvest season (August–September), as popular wineries fill quickly.
Be mindful that the Golan Heights has a mixed Jewish, Druze, and Christian population; dress modestly in villages and ask permission before photographing people or homes.
Plan transportation carefully—designate a driver or use taxis/tours, as roads wind through hills and designated driving under wine influence laws are strictly enforced.

restaurants.name: Dag Al HaDan
restaurants.description: Lakeside kibbutz restaurant famous for fresh fish from the Sea of Galilee with stunning water views.

restaurants.name: Pundak HaGolan
restaurants.description: Casual local favorite serving hearty Golan specialties in a rustic setting with panoramic views.
restaurants.mustOrder: Golan lamb stew with local herbs

restaurants.name: Rauchberger Winery Restaurant
restaurants.description: Wine estate restaurant offering refined Mediterranean cuisine paired with award-winning local Golan wines.

restaurants.name: Mitzpe Hayamim Kibbutz Restaurant
restaurants.description: Upscale kibbutz dining with creative Mediterranean dishes overlooking the Galilee landscape.
restaurants.mustOrder: Goat cheese and walnut crusted fish

restaurants.name: Ortal Restaurant
restaurants.description: Contemporary Israeli grill house with quality meats and seasonal vegetables in casual-modern setting.

restaurants.name: Bustan Restaurant
restaurants.description: Traditional Levantine cuisine in warm atmosphere, known for authentic flavors and generous portions.

restaurants.name: Katzrin Brewery & Steakhouse
restaurants.description: Modern steakhouse with on-site craft brewery featuring premium beef and house-brewed beers.
restaurants.mustOrder: Prime ribeye with local craft IPA

restaurants.name: Dag Ganuz
restaurants.description: Contemporary fish restaurant at kibbutz Ein Gev offering creative seafood preparations with lakeside ambiance.
restaurants.mustOrder: Grilled sea bass with olive tapenade

restaurants.name: Hakufa Restaurant
restaurants.description: Family-friendly establishment serving well-executed Israeli and Mediterranean comfort food.

restaurants.name: Tal's Restaurant
restaurants.description: Simple, honest Golan cuisine at modest prices in a no-frills kibbutz setting.

restaurants.name: Moran's Vineyard Restaurant
restaurants.description: Fine dining vineyard restaurant emphasizing Golan wine pairings with seasonal Mediterranean menus.
restaurants.mustOrder: Aged beef with wine-braised root vegetables

restaurants.name: Kibbutz Merom Golan Dining Hall
restaurants.description: Authentic kibbutz communal dining with traditional Israeli fare and warm hospitality.

restaurants.name: El Romani
restaurants.description: Italian-Mediterranean fusion restaurant with homemade pasta and local produce emphasis.
restaurants.mustOrder: Fresh pappardelle with wild boar ragù

restaurants.name: Dag Nof Sukkah
restaurants.description: Casual sukkah-style fish restaurant at kibbutz with grilled catch and scenic Galilee views.
